The Serpentspine underworld is a whole second map plane of 385 sunless provinces, where a fixed 14-hour lamplit shift makes dwarven toil out-produce a full surface day.
There is no sun underground, so cavern colonies farm cave-fungus grown on the bare rock floor: meager food, but the ore-rich stone repays the miners.
The tiger-folk Harimari of Anbennar mature fast: their children enter the workforce at age nine, where a human child must wait until fifteen.
Only human names are hand-authored into CivStudio; every other race's names are generated on demand from Anbennar's own culture files and cached.
The calendar is pre-Reformation: Sunday is the only weekly rest day, Saturday is a workday, and 31 fixed feasts outrank even a Sunday.
On a feast day every commoner firm shuts and only the nobles labor, because the strategic export sector works straight through the holy days.
People age and die on Coale-Demeny West Level 3 life tables, a real historical mortality schedule whose life expectancy at birth is just twenty-five years.
Every person carries twelve RimWorld-style skills with passions; a skill you are passionate about never fades, while unused mastery slowly rusts back toward competence.
Daylight literally scales your labor output, computed by a vendored sunrise-sunset calculator; near London's midsummer the astronomical twilight can last the entire night.
Dead laborers are not inherited: the ruler feeds a pool of peasants and promotes the ablest to fill each empty household, making replacement meritocratic rather than hereditary.
Ruler colonies are designed to collapse: the labor model only replaces the dead one-for-one, so every settlement eventually winds down as its reserve drains.
There are no founding nobles at all; the entire aristocracy is raised from the ablest laborers, re-banked from copper into silver upon their elevation.
Wealth climbs a currency ladder: commoners bank in copper, nobles in silver, rulers in gold, and money-changers skim a fee at every exchange.
Caravans march like a Roman army, and a band so long that it spends the whole day filing out of camp makes zero net progress.
A wandering band forages food and mines ore as it marches, yet a medieval caravan literally cannot see an oil or uranium deposit it lacks the tech to know.
The tech tree is 338 nodes lifted from Caveman2Cosmos, the Prehistoric-to-Renaissance slice carved out of that mod's sprawling 943-technology graph.
The world is the whole imported Anbennar map: 5,264 provinces nested inside 1,375 areas, 153 regions and 29 super-regions.
Same seed, same story: economy, naming, mortality, skills and terrain each ride their own salted random stream so no draw ever disturbs another.
Rivers are read straight from Anbennar's river bitmap, which encodes water width by color and even marks each source, confluence and split.
Anbennar's magical trade goods get their own class, where damestear, mithril, precursor relics and serpentbloom stand apart from mere grain and iron.
Explore through nine continuous zoom bands, from a 1x EU4-style strategy atlas all the way down to a 256x city-builder view of building lots.
Some forests are stranger than others: crimson blood-magic bloodgroves, teal fey gladeways and pale glaciers each get their own terrain, art and yields.
Your privy council is staffed by real nobles from the leader's court — the ablest scholar heads Technology, and when one dies a successor quietly takes the seat.
Every advisor wears their own race's Anbennar portrait; a tiger-folk Harimari councillor really does look like a tiger in a silk robe.
